intro: Everything we’ve learned raising rare anthurium in Saigon — the exact envelope, the medium, and the small daily habits that turn a fussy aroid into velvet perfection.

sections.title: The Purple Envelope
sections.body:
Rare anthurium reward **stability** over everything. We hold the room inside a tight window and let the plant settle into it.

- **Temperature:** steady 24–26°C, day and night. Avoid swings and cold drafts.
- **Humidity:** 70–85% RH. Pair high humidity with gentle airflow so leaves never sit wet.
- **Light:** bright, indirect — about 100–150 µmol PAR, ~12-hour photoperiod. No harsh direct sun.
- **Airflow:** a constant soft breeze prevents fungal issues and strengthens petioles.

Chasing fast growth is the #1 mistake. Slow and stable gives you the quilted velvet and silver veins collectors chase.

sections.title: Medium & Potting
sections.body:
Anthurium roots want **air**. They rot in dense, wet soil.

- Use a chunky, airy aroid mix: bark, perlite/pumice, and horticultural charcoal. Many growers add a little sphagnum for moisture balance.
- Choose pots with strong drainage. Clear nursery pots help you watch roots and moisture.
- **Semi-hydro (LECA/Pon)** is an excellent, low-rot option once a plant is established — keep a shallow reservoir and a consistent nutrient solution.
- Re-pot when roots fill the pot or the mix breaks down (usually every 12–18 months).

sections.title: Watering
sections.body:
Aim for **evenly moist, never soggy**.

- Let the top of the mix dry slightly between waterings; never let the plant sit in standing water.
- Use room-temperature, low-mineral water (rain/RO/filtered) when possible — velvet leaves dislike hard water spots.
- In high humidity, plants drink slower; water by feel and weight, not by calendar.

sections.title: Feeding
sections.body:
Feed **little and often**.

- A balanced fertilizer at roughly ¼–½ strength, every 1–2 weeks during active growth.
- Flush the medium with plain water monthly to prevent salt build-up.
- Ease off in cooler, lower-light periods.

sections.title: Acclimating a New Arrival
sections.body:
Plants shipped internationally arrive stressed and often bare-root. Go slow.

1. Unbox immediately, rinse roots, and rehydrate in clean water for 20–30 minutes.
2. Pot into your airy mix (or sphagnum to recover roots).
3. First 2 weeks: **higher humidity, lower light**, no fertilizer. A clear humidity tent or prop box helps.
4. Quarantine away from your collection for ~2 weeks and watch for pests.
5. Expect the first new leaf to be small — that’s normal. Stability brings the next ones up to size.

sections.title: Pests & Troubleshooting
sections.body:
Inspect weekly — early action is everything.

- **Thrips:** silvery streaks, deformed new growth. Isolate, rinse, treat repeatedly.
- **Spider mites:** fine webbing, stippling — thrive in dry air; raise humidity and treat.
- **Root rot:** mushy, dark roots from staying wet. Trim to healthy tissue, re-pot airy, ease watering.
- **Crispy edges:** humidity too low or salts too high — raise RH, flush the medium.
- **Yellowing:** usually overwatering or old leaves; check roots first.

sections.title: Notes by Specimen
sections.body:
**Carlablackiae** — near-black velvet, slow and proud. Maximum stability; protect the velvet from water spots.

**Stripey (variegata)** — variegated, unstable by nature; give a touch more light to hold pattern, but never direct sun on pale sectors.

**Red Crystallinum (red form)** — blushes blood-red on new growth, hardening to green with silver veins. Loves humidity and steady warmth.

title: Welcome to the Purple Room
excerpt: How one velvet leaf turned into a climate-controlled atelier of rare anthurium in the heart of Saigon.
body:
It started with a single **Carlablackiae** leaf.

What began as a windowsill obsession is now a climate-controlled room held at 26°C and 70–85% humidity, where the rarest aroids are grown like living jewellery.

In this journal I'll share grow notes, new unfurls, restocks and the stories behind each specimen. Welcome to the veinclusive purple selection.

title: Caring for Carlablackiae — the grail, demystified
excerpt: Velvet, bullate and notoriously fussy — here's the exact envelope that keeps Carla pushing perfect hearts.
body:
Carlablackiae rewards consistency.

- **Light:** bright indirect / ~120 µmol PAR, 12h photoperiod
- **Humidity:** 70–85% RH, gentle airflow
- **Medium:** chunky, airy aroid mix — never wet feet
- **Temp:** steady 24–26°C

The biggest mistake is chasing fast growth. Slow, stable conditions give you the quilted velvet and silver veins collectors chase for a decade.
